## [06:05 - 09:05]

Here's really where it all starts. And it's these two models. So the classical way of doing consulting and everything called consulting coaching services and all of that is the centralised consulting model. And most consultants start out using a centralised control model with them at the centre of everything. And this includes clients, contractors, billing support, etc. And this is the best model to use when starting out because you can oversee everything yourself. It's fast, it's simple and you don't need to rely on anything else. The problem arises when you start to add more clients, contractors and processes to the system with you still at the centre. And eventually with enough scale, this model will break and or the consultant will burn out. So the way to understand this model is you're the red dot and all these other dots out here, they're all different clients or contractors or tasks or whatever. And for anything to happen, it has to come through you. So if a contractor needs some information from a client, well that's got to relay through you. The contractor can't go to the client, the client can't go to the contractor, I mean it's just all you in the middle. And everything's coming through you. A support question, a billing question, just absolutely everything is coming through you. That's the centralised consulting model. And this is the decentralised consulting model. So this is what I have really been pushing with, you know, consulting.com and my training programmes is really decentralising the consulting business model and making it like this. So for a consulting business to scale successfully, it must switch from a centralised model to a decentralised model. The decentralised model means that the consultant is no longer the only resource for information. There are multiple sources for information and both clients and contractors can go to these sources and get what they need without interrupting the consultant. And most people think that this means hiring people, but that's not the only way. Well, this is the definition of a business workflow. So a workflow is a progression of steps that could be tasks, events, interactions, anything that make up a work process and creates or adds value to the business. And in a sequential workflow, each step is dependent on the occurrence of the previous step. And in a parallel workflow, two or more steps can occur at the same time.

## [18:11 - 21:12]

So when you're creating a workflow for your business, you want to think about things like onboarding clients. If you're in the done for you business, then onboarding clients is a bit of a process. You need to get information from them. You need to talk to them. You need to get all of this stuff together. And then when they're on board, you need to set them all up, deliver the work. I mean, there's a lot of processes involved. And the worst thing to do is to just wing it every time. Because if you just wing it every time, something's bound to break, and the results you get from it are bound to be unpredictable, because every time it's different. So once you've really figured out what works for you, you want to map out your workflow. And this is how you do it. And the difference between a sequential one and a parallel one, well, a sequential one means that each step has to come after the other step. So for example, if someone needed to start a business, they would probably need something to sell before they started selling it. That's just very basic. One thing has to come before the other. However, if it was like redesigning the website and redoing the office, I mean, those things can happen at the same time. So you could have two flows happening. And that's the difference between sequential and parallel. And you're probably thinking, why do we need workflows? Doesn't this sound a bit too formal or a bit too like corporatey for us running our little businesses? Well, here's why you need them. So when you're a consultant providing done for you services or advice, it's a good idea to map out your key processes and workflows so that you can remember what to do every time and keep the results consistent. And even though some pilots have flown over 10,000 hours and know everything about flying in the aircraft like the back of their hand, they still follow simple checklists and workflows every time they take off, fly, and land. Every single time. Good pilots will follow these all the time because they just don't want to mess anything up and they know that human error is a huge thing. And by creating workflows, we remove the human error from the process and we help eliminate errors and we improve the speed and make the outcome and result more predictable. And these are all things that we want in our consulting business. So if we've got a process that works, we want to stick to it. Now if we're trying to improve the process, sure, we want to try and iterate a few things. But when it works and we just want to stick to it and just keep rolling it out, we just want to leave it the same. you haven't got a refined process that you're implementing for people and getting them results with, then don't worry about mapping out a workflow. It's not going to help you at all. All you are doing is selling. And then on Thursday on Friday, you're not doing any selling anymore. Now all you're doing is delivering, service delivery.

So start learning to block out entire days. You know, it takes your brain three hours to get into gear. So when I start working on something in the morning, let's say I start at 9 o'clock.
